title: L1 Regularization
concept: L1 regularization pushes some weak weights all the way to zero, creating a sparse network that keeps only a smaller set of active connections.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Compared with no penalty, what does L2 weight decay usually do to weights?

options.text: It shrinks them smoothly, especially the largest ones.
It turns every weight into a bias.
It adds new hidden layers during training.

exercises.explain: Exactly. L2 gently pulls weights downward, which sets up a nice contrast with L1's more pruning-oriented style.

cards.title: Every shelf must earn its space
cards.body: Imagine cleaning a tiny apartment. When space is limited, weakly useful items do not just get pushed closer to the wall — some get removed completely. L1 regularization treats weak connections that way.

cards.title: What L1 regularization does
cards.body: L1 regularization adds a penalty based on the absolute size of weights. In practice, that creates a constant push toward zero, so some small or weak connections get driven all the way to exactly zero, making the network sparser.

cards.title: Pruning a tree
cards.body: A gardener does not shorten every branch by the same gentle amount forever. Some thin, unhelpful branches get cut off completely so the tree can focus on the strongest structure. L1 acts like that pruning shears.

cards.title: Dropping weak word detectors
cards.body: Suppose a text classifier has thousands of tiny word-detection connections. L1 can push weak detectors for irrelevant words all the way to zero, while keeping stronger connections for truly helpful topic words active.

cards.title: Sparse does not always mean best
cards.body: It is tempting to think a network with more zero weights must be smarter. Not always. Sometimes lots of small clues really do matter together, and pruning too aggressively can throw away useful signal.

cards.title: Dense vs. sparse connections
cards.body: L1 stands out because it can truly switch some connections off.

left.text:
Many tiny connections stay alive.
More paths remain active.

right.text:
Some weak connections hit exactly zero.
Only a smaller set of routes stays active.

exercises.prompt: What special behavior is L1 regularization known for?

options.text: It can push some weights to exactly zero.
It makes every weight large and positive.
It removes the need for hidden layers.
It guarantees the lowest possible training loss.

exercises.explain: Yes. L1 is famous for encouraging exact zeros, which creates sparsity.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a weight becomes zero under L1 regularization, that connection is effectively turned off.
exercises.explain: True. A zero weight contributes nothing through that connection, so it is effectively inactive.

exercises.sentence: L1 regularization often creates a ___ network with fewer active connections.
exercises.explain: Sparse is correct. L1 tends to leave a smaller set of non-zero connections.

exercises.prompt: Which weight pattern looks most like L1 regularization?
exercises.explain: Correct. L1 often leaves a pattern with several exact zeros and a few surviving connections.

exercises.prompt: You are training a keyword detector with thousands of candidate signals, but you want a smaller network where many weak connections disappear entirely so later pruning is easier. Which approach fits best?

options.text: Use L1 regularization so weak connections are encouraged to hit exactly zero.
Use only a larger learning rate so weights jump around more wildly.
Keep all weights large so no clue gets ignored.
Turn off the validation set and judge only training accuracy.

exercises.explain: Exactly. L1 is the regularizer most closely associated with sparse, zero-heavy weight patterns that make the network easier to simplify.
